title: Aggregate Functions

overview.what:
Aggregate functions take a set of rows and return a single scalar value. The five core aggregates are COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, and MAX.

- COUNT(*) counts all rows; COUNT(column) counts non-NULL values in that column.
- SUM and AVG work on numeric columns and ignore NULLs.
- MIN and MAX work on any orderable type (numbers, strings, dates).

Without GROUP BY, an aggregate collapses the entire table into one row. With GROUP BY, each group gets its own aggregate result.

Against the shared dataset:

  SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_products,
         AVG(price) AS avg_price,
         MIN(price) AS cheapest,
         MAX(price) AS most_expensive
  FROM products;

Returns: 6 | 85.82333... | 4.99 | 199.99 — one summary row from six product rows.

Think of aggregates as squeezers: they take a bag of values and squeeze out one number.
overview.why: Aggregates are the building blocks of every business metric — total revenue, average order size, highest salary, number of active users. Without them you can only list individual rows, never summarize.

why_learning_this: Computing any business metric — totals, averages, counts — requires aggregate functions
Understanding the difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(column) prevents subtle NULL-related bugs

common_mistakes.title: Using COUNT(*) when you mean COUNT(column)
common_mistakes.explanation: COUNT(*) counts all rows including those where the column of interest is NULL. COUNT(country) on the customers table returns 7 (skipping Emma's NULL country), while COUNT(*) returns 8. Use COUNT(column) when you want to count non-NULL values only.

common_mistakes.title: Using SUM when you mean COUNT
common_mistakes.explanation: SUM adds up the values in a numeric column; COUNT counts how many rows exist. SUM(quantity) from order_items gives the total units sold (19), while COUNT(*) gives the number of line items (11). Mixing these up produces nonsensical metrics.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting that AVG ignores NULLs
common_mistakes.explanation: AVG skips NULL values entirely — it divides by the count of non-NULL values, not by the total row count. If 3 out of 5 rows have values 10, 20, 30 and 2 are NULL, AVG returns 20 (sum 60 / 3 non-NULLs), not 12 (60 / 5). Use COALESCE to turn NULLs into 0 if you want them included in the denominator.

recall_questions.q: What is the difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(column)?
recall_questions.answer: COUNT(*) counts all rows regardless of NULLs. COUNT(column) counts only the rows where that column is not NULL.

recall_questions.q: What happens when you use an aggregate function without GROUP BY?
recall_questions.answer: The entire table is treated as one group, and the query returns a single row with the aggregated result.

recall_questions.q: How does AVG handle NULL values?
recall_questions.answer: AVG ignores NULLs — it sums the non-NULL values and divides by the count of non-NULL values, not the total row count.

understanding_checks.question: What does this query return?
understanding_checks.why: COUNT(*) counts all 8 customer rows. COUNT(country) skips Emma (id 5) whose country is NULL, returning 7. This is the classic difference between the two COUNT forms.

understanding_checks.query:
SELECT category,
       SUM(price) AS total_price,
       COUNT(*) AS product_count
FROM products
GROUP BY category
ORDER BY category;
understanding_checks.question: What rows does this return?
understanding_checks.why: Products are grouped by category. Electronics: Keyboard(49.99) + Mouse(19.99) + Monitor(199.99) = 269.97, 3 products. Furniture: Desk(149.99) + Chair(89.99) = 239.98, 2 products. Stationery: Notebook(4.99), 1 product.

query_walkthrough.query:
SELECT category,
       COUNT(*) AS num_products,
       MIN(price) AS cheapest,
       MAX(price) AS priciest
FROM products
GROUP BY category
ORDER BY category;
query_walkthrough.focus: Watch 6 product rows collapse into 3 category groups, each summarised by COUNT, MIN, and MAX.

practice_tasks.title: Add SUM and AVG to the summary
practice_tasks.prompt: This query shows COUNT and MIN per category. Add a SUM(price) column aliased as total_price and an AVG(price) column aliased as avg_price. Keep the existing columns and ordering.
practice_tasks.starter_code:
SELECT category,
       COUNT(*) AS num_products,
       MIN(price) AS cheapest
FROM products
GROUP BY category
ORDER BY category;
practice_tasks.solution:
SELECT category,
       COUNT(*) AS num_products,
       MIN(price) AS cheapest,
       SUM(price) AS total_price,
       AVG(price) AS avg_price
FROM products
GROUP BY category
ORDER BY category;

challenge.title: Total quantity sold per product
challenge.prompt: Write a query that shows each product name and the total quantity sold across all order items. Only include products that appear in order_items. Order by total quantity descending, then by name.
challenge.solution:
SELECT p.name, SUM(oi.quantity) AS total_qty
FROM products p
JOIN order_items oi ON oi.product_id = p.id
GROUP BY p.name
ORDER BY total_qty DESC, p.name;
